Coronal	  plane	  goes	  through	  skull,	  pelvis,	  abdomen	  -­‐	  just	  anterior	  to	  spine,	  femur	  and	   ball	  of	  foot.	   o Erector	  spinae	  muscles	  balance	  the	  torques	  making	  us	  fall	  forward.	   	   Effect	  of	  Posture	   • We	  can	  move	  our	  centre	  of	  mass	  by	  moving	  our	  body/limbs	  ie	  change  harder	  to	  maintain	  with	  a	  higher	  centre	  of	  mass	  or	   smaller	  base	  of	  support)	  	   • Via	  feedback	  from	  senses	  concerning	  the	  bodies	  position	   o Visual,	  vestibular	  and	  proprioception	   • We	  balance	  by	  shifting	  our	  centre	  of	  mass	  constantly	   • Typically	  the	  arms	  are	  thrown	  out	  to	  maintain	  balance,	  as	  this	  quickly	  shifts	  the	  centre	  of	  mass	  	   	   	   Centre of mass, posture, balance Page 2 of 4 This means that once a high diver leaves the tower it is impossible for them to rotate about any point other than their centre of mass. CENTRE OF MASS OF THE BODY For a person standing erect with good posture the centre of mass is approximately given by: In the anterior plane, C of M is approximately at waist level. In the sagittal plane, a vertical line through the C of M goes through: centre of mass of the skull centre of rib cage centre of pelvis axis of femur ball of foot in front of ankle Note that the centre of mass is usually in front of the spinal column. This means that if we consider the upper body as being supported by the spine at its point of attachment to the pelvis then the weight of the body creates a torque about this point of attachment. Equilibrium can only occur if there is an equal and opposite torque elsewhere. A large part of this torque arises from muscular force applied by the back muscles (erector spinae). We will consider the effects of this in the next lecture. Note that the centre of mass must always be over the support for a freely standing body. Why should this be so? If not then the body will rotate about the point of support since the supporting force and the force of gravity (ie the person's weight) will exert a torque about any chosen point. This can be seen in the adjacent diagram where it is clear that the torque about any point cannot be zero. The automatic consequence of this is that the person will not be in equilibrium and will begin to rotate about the support. That is, they will start to fall over. The higher the centre of mass and the smaller the area of support the more difficult it is to maintain a position of equilibrium. Thus it is more difficult to stand on a taut wire than to stand on the ground. It would in fact be easier to sit on the wire since your centre of mass is nearer to the point of support.
